channel.title: Bloomberg Green Global
channel.description: Últimos artigos de Bloomberg Green Global

item.title: US Northeast Threatened by Severe Thunderstorms, Damaging Winds
item.description:
More than 63 million Americans in a Northeast Corridor that spans Washington, through New York, to Boston are threatened by wild weather on Thursday, including severe thunderstorms and damaging winds.

Power outages and travel disruptions are likely, while there’s even a chance a tornado could develop across upstate New York or New England. A wider area stretching from Texas to Maine, and beyond into Canada, is also at risk from potentially dangerous storms, weather agencies in both countries said.

The Northeast is in the firing line after Tropical Storm Arthur — the first named storm of the 2026 Atlantic season — brought “life-threatening flooding” to the southeastern US on Wednesday. Arthur has degenerated into a low-pressure area along the upper Texas coast, but there’s still a chance of gusty winds, hail and possibly even a tornado across the Gulf Coast.

As of 5:30 a.m. New York time, there were tornado watches in parts of Kentucky, West Virginia, and Ohio. There are also flashflood warnings in the area as thunderstorm-related downpours sweep through the region, the National Weather Service said.

Across upstate New York and most of Vermont there are wind advisories, with gusts of up to 50 miles (80 kilometers) per hour capable of downing tree limbs and causing power outages.
item.dc:creator: Brian K Sullivan

item.title: US West Set to Sizzle This Summer While Northeast Stays Cooler
item.description:
The US West and Gulf Coast are set to broil this summer while the rest of the nation may face dreary and cool days — thanks in part to El Niño.

The US Climate Prediction Center sees the western and southern US hot through July 10, with the Upper Great Lakes region and parts of the Northeast set for below-normal temperatures.

The forecast owes much of its pattern to conditions across the Pacific Ocean, including the emergence of El Niño. The US declared the phenomenon underway last week, with conditions gathering strength and raising risks of heat waves, severe drops in rainfall and flooding in parts of the world.

“Usually in developing El Niño summers it tends to be hot in the Northwest, not hot in the Midwest and Northeast,” said Jonathan Erdman, senior meteorologist for the Weather Channel app, which is owned by the Weather Company LLC. “So, this is kind of acting according to script so far.”

Read More: Why the World Is Bracing for a Rare ‘Super’ El Niño

The locked-in pattern means dryness will likely continue across the western US, raising water supply issues for many regions that have been starved by last winter’s snow drought. The threat of wildfires across the region will rise as well, especially for Northern California, Oregon and Washington east to Colorado, according to the National Interagency Fire Center.

Meanwhile, cooler temperatures in large cities in the Midwest and Northeast will also limit energy demand. The milder outlook for those regions prompted the Commodity Weather Group to drop 1.1 cooling degree days — a measurement of how temperature affects energy demand — off its forecast on Wednesday.

June is coming in cooler than four of the last five for the eastern US, said Matt Rogers, president of the Commodity Weather Group. Overall, the eastern US will probably be close to normal through the summer, but conditions in the Indian Ocean may actually push the region to become a bit warmer.

North Pacific marine heat waves are also playing a role in this summer’s forecast by influencing weather patterns, Jennifer Francis, a senior scientist at the Woodwell Climate Research Center, said in an interview.

“These heat waves have been expanding and intensifying in the past couple of decades thanks to human-caused warming, but their location in the Pacific shifts around each year,” she said. “For the past several months, a blob of warmer-than-normal water has been parked west of Mexico and Southern California.”

The resulting high temperatures build and soils dry out from California to Colorado, which amplifies the heat, according to Francis. This pattern also spurs on severe thunderstorms, hail and tornadoes, which have been plaguing the US from Chicago to Washington, DC for the past week and New York on Thursday.
item.dc:creator: Brian K Sullivan

item.title: India Monsoon Starts Almost 40% Short as El Niño Curbs Rains
item.description:
India’s monsoon has had an unusually weak start and the lackluster rainfall is likely to persist, threatening crops and industrial activity as the El Niño weather phenomenon takes hold.

Nationwide monsoon rainfall was nearly 40% below normal as of Wednesday, according to the latest data from the India Meteorological Department. The season runs from June through September and is responsible for the bulk of the country’s annual rainfall. The poor start is hampering the growing season for staples from rice to soybeans, as well as disrupting key sectors like construction.

It’s one of the first indicators of how El Niño — which recently emerged — is beginning to upend global weather norms. The climate pattern is historically associated with dryness in South Asia, and US scientists expect it to be one of the strongest on record. Weather models are currently predicting abnormal dryness to persist in India through July and August, particularly over its northwest and central regions.

“We do not currently see a signal strong enough to recover the existing national rainfall deficit back toward normal,” said Takahisa Nishikawa, senior decision support meteorologist at Atmospheric G2. “The expected improvement looks more like a temporary or regional pickup rather than a full-scale normalization of the monsoon.”

Read More: Why the World Is Bracing for a Rare ‘Super’ El Niño: Explainer.

Monsoonal rains that sweep across India are crucial for the country’s agriculture and energy industries. The country is one of the world’s largest producers of rice, sugar and cotton, and farmers depend on the seasonal deluges to irrigate their fields. Poor harvests raise the risk that authorities could impose export restrictions.

Mumbai’s water authority also recently suspended supplies to construction sites, the first such intervention in 12 years on developers. The agency has reduced distribution to business, factories and sports clubs stopped supplying to swimming pools.

Read More: Dry June Forces Mumbai to Shut Taps for Construction Sites.

There may be slight improvements to rainfall next week, helped by moist southwesterly winds that will allow the monsoon to continue advancing north into early July, but broader conditions remain unfavorable beyond that period, Nishikawa said.

“The poor monsoon start is already showing up in our crop-weather alerts, with new severe dry alerts for India soybean and groundnut areas,” said Fergus Keatinge, vice president at Marcus Weather Inc. The unseasonal dryness may persist into July, likely delaying soybean seeding and shortening the growing season, he added.

Farmers are in the midst of the biggest planting period of the year, and crop conditions have time to recover if rains pick up soon. Still, official forecasts point toward the weakest monsoon in more than a decade.

“The developing strong El Niño is a key driver, as it raises the risk of a weaker and more uneven Indian monsoon,” Keatinge said.

The southwest monsoon is set to advance into more parts of the states of Telangana, Odisha, Jharkhand, Bihar and Chhattisgarh over the next four to five days, according to the India Meteorological Department. Rainfall is likely to remain below normal across most of India next week, except in parts of southern peninsular and the Northeast regions, where it is expected to be near normal, it said.

Conditions will likely become favorable for the monsoon to extend into parts of Maharashtra, Gujarat, Madhya Pradesh and Uttar Pradesh, as well as the remaining areas of the central Arabian Sea, during the week beginning June 25, the weather office said. Rains are expected to be below normal nationwide, except in Konkan and Goa, Karnataka, Rayalaseema, Kerala and Tamil Nadu, where it may be near normal to above normal during the period, it said.

To get Bloomberg’s Weather Watch newsletter in your inbox, click here.
item.dc:creator: Mary Hui

item.title: The Energy Transition Debate We Need to Have
item.description:
The key to tackling climate change is moving away from burning fossil fuels to using renewable energy. But in his book More and More and More, French historian Jean-Baptiste Fressoz argues that the world has never actually managed a successful energy transition before and current plans are unrealistic.

Many have taken his writing to mean that stopping global warming is impossible, however, he tells Akshat Rathi this week on Zero, his view is actually quite different.

Listen now, and subscribe on Apple, Spotify, or YouTube to get new episodes of Zero every Thursday.

Our transcripts are generated by a combination of software and human editors, and may contain slight differences between the text and audio. Please confirm in audio before quoting in print.

Akshat Rathi

You’ve heard of the energy transition right? It features in speeches from world leaders, oil company CEOs and even the heads of central banks. These are not your typical climate activists and, yet, they all talk about this phenomenon. This is Zero. I’m Akshat Rathi. And this week: why does everybody have strong feelings about the energy transition?

Simply put, the energy transition is the idea that the world has moved from one fuel to another in the past. From wood to coal and then from coal to oil. Now, to tackle climate change, it must move from oil to renewables.

On this show, we talk plenty about the rise of renewables. But Jean-Baptiste Fressoz doesn’t think there’s an energy transition happening. He is a historian at the French National Centre for Scientific Research and he says the world has never really transitioned from one energy source to another.

In his book More and More and More, he shows that the world isn’t burning a lot less wood than it did before the industrial revolution. If anything, every new source of fuel has just added to the growing pile of energy consumption. There is, he says, no transition… just addition.

In my job speaking to energy experts, I’ve found that some have absorbed that conclusion to mean an energy transition can never happen. That there is no way to tackle climate change. When I show those climate delayers that we have seen energy transitions at a country level many times, they say go read Jean-Baptiste Fressoz.

And so I did and I wanted to bring Jean-Baptiste on the show to discuss whether he agrees with the many fans of his book. As you’ll hear, his view is far more nuanced. And his goal in exposing what he calls the “false history” of the energy transition is to get the world to work harder on tackling climate change. He wants us to be more radical at cutting fossil fuel use, not give up.

Akshat Rathi

Welcome to the show, John Baptiste.

Jean-Baptiste Fressoz

Thanks for having me.

Akshat Rathi

Well, I enjoyed reading More and More and More. It came out a couple of years ago, but it's newly relevant given what's happening in the world right now. But the central thesis in your book is that the phrase ‘energy transition’, in a way, is a gimmick. It's a marketing idea. It's kind of propaganda. And the world has never really transitioned away from one fuel source to another.

And as a polemic, trying to reframe the conversation that the world is having right now about an energy transition, it does a great job. But as with any polemic, there are other views that we should address. But before we get there, and before we have a debate about that, let's start with the history. As a historian, you do this fantastic job of explaining where the idea of an energy transition came from in the first place. Tell me about Palmer Cosslett Putnam. What was his role?

Jean-Baptiste Fressoz

He’s a consultant for the Atomic Energy Commission in the US. And he was also a great innovator for wind power, for industrial wind power in the middle of the 20th century. He wrote a really fantastic report on the future of energy. And this report was written for the Atomic Energy Commission. This report is extraordinarily innovative in many different regards.

In 1953, in this report, you probably find the first world global quantitative history of energy. Putnam shows that the biggest producer of energy until the 1830s was India, not Britain. And then it was replaced not by Britain, of the coal and the steam power, but by the US of wood. So it has really a new vision and much more quantitative vision of the history of energy.

It is also very innovative because they talk about climate change, which is quite remarkable in 1953. And it is true that climate change is a powerful argument in favour of nuclear energy. So the argument was that there are limits for the use of fossil fuels. It is the limitation of the geological resource, but is also the amount of CO2 that you can pump into the atmosphere. And you got a fantastic graph in this report where you can see the rise of the level of the Pacific and Atlantic Oceans on both sides of the United States. And of course, it is a global phenomenon and is linked to the greenhouse effect and the burning of fossil fuels.

And Putnam is also part of this group of atomic lobbyists who want to show that in the future, there will be a massive change in the energy system. And he talks about a transition to nuclear energy. It's not yet the phrase ‘energy transition’, which will come a little bit after. But there is also this idea that in a century or two, the US and even the world will have to make a kind of transition out of fossil fuels towards nuclear energy. Fossil fuel will be just used as raw material, basically, to make steel, for instance.

Akshat Rathi

And this idea is kind of logical, right? Fossil fuels are limited. At some point, we will burn them. And of course, climate change is a thing that will get worse if we keep burning fossil fuels. And so atomic scientists are saying, well, the solution is to have a transition to nuclear power. This is the 1950s, 1960s, academics are talking in their circles. There are some people at the government level that are talking about it. But it's really the 1970s oil crises that take the idea of the energy transition all the way to the US president. How does that happen?

Jean-Baptiste Fressoz

What is difficult to imagine is that energy transition is really completely absent from energy discourse in the 1960s. Very few people talk about energy transition. Only nuclear scientists talk about energy transition. By the way, the phrase ‘energy transition’ is first and foremost a term of nuclear physics. It designates a shift of an electron around its nucleus. So it is really a phrase which comes from atomic physics, which will be used by nuclear lobbies to explain in the future we'll have to do something.

But this future is a really faraway future. For them, the transition will take place over three or four centuries. It has nothing to do with the transition we have to do today, which is more or less in three or four decades, instead of three or four centuries. And it is true that the decade of the 1970s is key for the diffusion of the discourse. The keyword at that time was ‘energy crisis’. It is on every talk show, radio, television. And energy transition becomes the kind of very general word to describe all the solutions to fight against the energy crisis. So in the 1970s, energy transition could mean more coal, for instance, which was exactly what the US government wanted to do in the 1970s, to develop coal to limit the effect of the rising price of oil.

And why is the decade of the 70s so crucial? It is also because at the same time, the issue of climate change emerged. It is at the end of the 1970s that this idea of ‘we have a serious problem with the rising concentration of carbon dioxide in the atmosphere’ became talked about in the political arenas, for instance at the Senate, at the US Senate. And the problem is that this idea of energy transition moved from the debate around energy crisis and energy scarcity. And we have to do an energy transition, but it can mean more coal or it can mean a very gradual shift over several centuries. This idea then colonised the discourse around climate change.

Akshat Rathi

And there is this speech that in 1977, the US President Jimmy Carter gives. An address to the nation on energy.

[President Jimmy Carter]

We must look back in the history to understand our energy problem. Twice in the last several hundred years there’s been a transition in the way people use energy. The first was about 200 years ago when we changed away from wood, which had provided about 90 percent of all fuel, to coal, which was much more efficient.

Akshat Rathi

And he talks about the transition from wood to coal and then from coal to oil and gas. And, instead of talking about nuclear power this time, he says we must transition from oil and gas to solar power.

[President Jimmy Carter]

Because we are now running out of gas and oil, we must prepare quickly for a third change to strict conservation and to the renewed use of coal and the permanent renewable energy sources like solar power.

Akshat Rathi

And it's a great aspiration. He put solar panels on the rooftop of the White House. But you frame that as a problematic message. Why?

Jean-Baptiste Fressoz

I mean, Jimmy Carter is probably responsible for the global diffusion of this idea of energy transition. Before 1977, very few people, very few experts, talked about energy transition. After Jimmy Carter's speech on 18th of April, 1977, energy transition is everywhere. You find it at the OECD. You find it in several newspapers in English language, but also in other languages. And also the environmentalists in the U.S. start to talk about energy transition. The same environmentalists who were against nuclear power, became in favour of a solar transition.

Jimmy Carter, when he talked about energy transition, interestingly enough, he mentioned history, this false history that you just mentioned. There was a shift in the U.S. from wood to coal and then from coal to oil. And now we have to do a third energy transition. You said it was solar, but actually in the report and in the national energy plan of Jimmy Carter, it was first and foremost coal. Solar would come after. There would be a kind of relaunch of the coal industry, especially the idea to liquefy coal, to make synthetic oil. But it is true that in the far future, the idea would be to move to solar energy and renewables in general.

This 1977 speech even changed, I think, the way people imagine the dynamics of materials and energies. Suddenly everything became malleable. Things could change quickly. For instance, Amory Lovins, a very influential environmentalist, was a member of Friends of the Earth, explained that the U.S. could do an energy transition out of fossil fuels and to renewables in less than 50 years. It's totally doable. Coal might be important in the next decade, but after four or five decades, the whole U.S. economy could be based on solar energy.

So there is really, I think, a new vision of the dynamics of energy with a very rapid shift, complete transformation, which was not the case before.

Akshat Rathi

But why is that problematic? That sounds great.

Jean-Baptiste Fressoz

It's great, but it's very naive because when you think about energy dynamics in a bit more serious way, you see that shifts take a lot of time. They are never complete and there are a lot of things that depend and will depend on fossil fuels for a far away future. I could mention steel, I could mention plastic, I could mention cement, I could mention aviation, maritime shipping. There are a lot of things that solar power is not really able to really decarbonise. So I think it was a very naive understanding of the dynamics of materials and energy.

Akshat Rathi

There was one particular chart that sort of shaped President Carter's view on this. I want to know why exactly it's hard, but let's just look at that chart and why that chart makes it possible to think that energy transition can happen. We'll put an image of the chart in the podcast transcript for readers to look at. But can you describe it?

Jean-Baptiste Fressoz

Yes, this chart represents the share of wood, the share of coal and the share of oil in the U.S. energy mix from 1860 to 1970. And when you put these energies in shares, suddenly you can make appear a beautiful transitions. Because wood energy was very important in 1860 and is not very important in terms of share for the production of energy in the U.S. in the 1950s, for instance. So it seemed that there was a beautiful transition from wood to coal.

And the same goes with coal and oil. Coal is extraordinarily important in 1900, but it's much less important in 1960. So it seems that there is a beautiful transition from coal to oil. The trick is to put everything in shares. But this is a new representation of energy. Most of the time at that epoch, the dynamics of energy were rather visualised through stack curves where you could see the fact that wood remains actually at the same level all across the 20th century.

That coal stays also very important until in the 1960s, obviously. And oil, of course, is getting more and more important from 1900 to 1960. So you got a completely different understanding of the dynamics of energy. And you see the history of energy rather had the history of accumulation, of energy addition and not energy transition.

Akshat Rathi

And that is what justifies the title that you have for the book, which is More and More and More, because in absolute amounts, most of these fuels we have actually just kept on increasing consumption, maybe flattened at some points, but really all forms of energy are just adding on top of each other. Now, this idea, though, does take root, that we can move from one energy source to the other.

And then climate change goes from the 1970s into the 1980s as this international agenda item being discussed at the United Nations. And the answer becomes, we should do an energy transition. And what will power that energy transition? Technologies that instead of using fossil fuels will use cleaner forms of energy. Can you explain why technologies of the past did not actually help us transition from one fuel to the other? We never really went from wood to coal and coal to oil.

Jean-Baptiste Fressoz

I mean, the very basic point that my book emphasised is that energies are intertwined in the economy. For instance, in the 19th century, industrialised countries used more and more wood to extract coal. To the point that Britain in the 20th century used more wood in the shape of pit props, timber mining, to extract coal than Britain had burned in the 18th century. So coal and wood are growing in symbiosis. They are not in competition.

In the same way, the idea that there is a transition from coal to oil in the 20th century is clearly wrong because oil mainly serves to power automobiles. And to make an automobile, you need a lot of steel and therefore a lot of coal. The calculations made for the case of Britain in the 1930s for each tonne of oil burnt in Britain, 2.5 tonnes of coal were needed to produce all the steel infrastructure and the automobiles that allowed the combustion of this oil. So really, coal and oil are growing together.

Of course, they are in competition in certain markets. For instance, it is true that steam engines were replaced by diesel engines, which are far more efficient and much less dangerous, obviously. But steam engines or diesel engines powered ships or trains made of steel and therefore consuming coal for their production. And there is such a growth in the economy in the 20th century that the use of coal just expands globally in the 20th century.

Akshat Rathi

I do want to come back to this idea that we really are stuck in having to use all forms of fuel, because there are places where transitions have happened. Before I come to that, let us finish the thought that you carry through, which is energy transition as an idea is created. The climate problem will be solved through an energy transition. Then there's one more layer in the 2000s, where you say alongside this framing, there is another framing that takes hold of policymakers.

And that framing is of climate as a resource that needs to be optimised. And it's the combination of the energy transition, with this optimisation framing that you say is why what we're doing right now to tackle climate change is the wrong thing. Can you explain that framing and why that's gotten us to a problem?

Jean-Baptiste Fressoz

Yes, I think basically economists were used to reflecting on the growing scarcity of oil in the 1970s. And what they proposed was a way to optimize the use of oil and fossil fuels in general. The best example of that is William Nordhaus. In 1973, he wrote a paper explaining that oil is expensive after the first oil shock. Does it mean that we need to conserve oil? On the contrary, it is better to burn it now while it is very valuable, because in 20 or 30 years, we will have the Breeder reactor. So we should not underestimate the power of disruptive innovation. Basically, that is his claim.

Two years later, he wrote the very first paper by an economist on climate change. And he made exactly the same point. There is an issue with global warming. Does it mean that we need to tighten our energy belt? Not at all. It would be so much easier to do the energy transition later, at the end of the 20th century, when we have the Breeder reactor and other technologies.

So this idea to make climate change cost-benefit, basically, to apply cost-benefit analysis on climate change and energy production is really grounded in this resource economics of the 1970s. And then it was applied to global climate change, to the planet in its entirety. And you can also see that the idea of energy transition served as a kind of delaying tactic in climate economics of the 1980s.

The argument was to say it would be cheaper to do it later. So it is better to do nothing now. I mean, the only thing we need to do is just do some R&D to be sure that the Breeder reactor will be ready when we really need to do the energy transition, when it really starts to become too warm.

Akshat Rathi

After the break, I ask Jean-Baptiste his solutions for a warming world. If you have feedback for the show, please email us at zeropod@bloomberg.net.

Akshat Rathi

Now let's come to the fuel side of the equation here, because it is true in absolute numbers that wood, coal and oil, in general, we either continue to consume the same amounts after reaching a peak, or we've continued to just increase the amount we consume. But in the book, you say that there is only one material that we have really actually reduced our consumption of, which is sheep's wool.

But there are other examples, whale oil, mercury, chlorofluorocarbons, asbestos, lead, almost all the materials that at least I could come up with to think about ones that we've moved away from, that we in absolute amounts use less of, are materials that cause environmental harms that far outweighed the cost of just using them for whatever we were using them. And so governments brought in regulation, and that forced companies to move to the alternative. Why do you think it cannot happen with carbon?

Jean-Baptiste Fressoz

I mean, first of all, let me be precise that I just said that sheep's wool was the only raw material where there was a decrease caused by kind of obsolescence, by technological obsolescence, because of synthetic fibres. It is true that asbestos and mercury have decreased very significantly after laws and global prohibitions because they were toxic materials.

So change is possible indeed, but we have to act much more decisively than what we do on fossil fuels. The idea that there could be a kind of technological obsolescence of coal, oil and gas in three decades, thanks to renewables or atomic energy, if you wish, is ludicrous. It's not based on any kind of empirical, historical understanding of the dynamics of energies and materials.

Akshat Rathi

But we have had transitions, right? We talk about energy transition as a thing that is typically slow, because if we look at it at a global scale it does take a long time for fuel mixes to change. But at a country level, we can see a transition happen. So here in the UK, there is no coal being burnt in power now. And soon, once the last steel furnace is converted into an electric one, there will be no coal being really burnt in the electric arc furnace in the steel plants here. If that can happen, then why are you worried that it's not based in physics? Because clearly it's possible, right?

Jean-Baptiste Fressoz

I mean, I think we should differentiate the electricity sector from all the rest. For the electricity sector, I think it makes sense to talk about transition. But many countries have already decarbonised their electricity system. There is nothing new in decarbonising an electric sector. You can mention Norway, Sweden, France, and many countries actually have over 80 percent renewables.

Akshat Rathi

Iceland, Costa Rica, Ethiopia, you know, there's a long list

Jean-Baptiste Fressoz

But like Brazil, for instance, has a massively decarbonised electricity system, but their emissions are growing, right? Because there is a lot of deforestation. So I think we should really differentiate between the electricity system, where transition could make sense, from the rest. The rest is much more complicated.

If you take the case of steel, you can put electric arc furnaces, but it means that you are recycling scrap metals. And it is much easier. So if Britain starts to consume much more scrap steel, it means that there will be less scrap steel for Turkey, for instance, which has a very big electric arc furnace steel industry, which perhaps means that somewhere else there will be more steel made from iron ore, and therefore emissions will be just transferred. So I think for steel, it's much, much more complicated than just going to electric arc furnaces.

Akshat Rathi

Yeah, but we know we can do it in the transport sector. So we are already seeing, for example, in places like Norway, oil consumption is starting to decline in an absolute amount because of the transition to electric cars. Now, 100% of the sales in Norway are electric. We're getting to like 60, 70% in many countries today. The most recent numbers from China in the month of May was 63% of all cars sold had a plug in it.

And so oil consumption can start to decline in a transition in a single country and eventually in the world too, right? So you could do electricity as a wedge to try and decarbonise other sectors of the economy. So maybe not all sectors, but electricity can take you very far in the process of the energy transition.

Jean-Baptiste Fressoz

That is true. But there are two points to be made. First, the electric car is certainly not zero carbon, because it is a big chunk of steel and steel will be made with coal or gas for a long time to come. We won't decarbonise the world steel industry in two or three decades. This is really quite an illusion. That's the first point. The second point is the aim, the goal, is not simply to diminish a little bit the emissions. It is to get to zero. Which is far, far more difficult. I think if the point was just to stabilize emissions or reduce it even by half, which is probably doable with the technologies we have, then I wouldn't be that worried.

The thing is, if you divide by half the global emissions, you just delay by two the rise of temperature. Climate change is a completely different challenge from an energy crisis, for instance. It is far more demanding.

Akshat Rathi

I think we can agree on that fact. BUt what happens is, one of the ways in which I was recommended your book is by people who I would class as climate delayers. They're like, ‘look, J.B. Fressoz has written this book that says we can just consume more and more and more and there is no energy transition. And what are you talking about addressing climate change? We just cannot do that.’ Whereas obviously your book has this more nuanced view that you can actually transition, you can reduce consumption of fossil fuels, maybe not to zero, but you could go very far along the way.

So if climate change is not just a problem of getting to zero, yes, of course, that is the final answer. But you have to first start reducing emissions, which the world can certainly do right now. So it's not something your book is saying, but that's what people take from the book, which is to take the idea that no energy transition has ever happened and thus, what are we even doing, thinking about addressing climate change?

Jean-Baptiste Fressoz

I think the book is rather about the vocabulary and the ideology that you have behind this idea of energy transition. With electric cars, what do you do? You don't do an energy transition. You decrease the carbon intensity of the economy, which is very different. In a country like France, you divide by two or three the carbon intensity of mobility, which is good, but it is far from enough. In a country like China, it is not as good because there is still a lot of coal in the electricity system.

So we should be really cautious about saying that the electric car is really the technology for the energy transition. When you think about the electric car at a global level, basically half of the electric cars are in China. And in China, 56% of electricity is still produced with coal. So basically what the electric car has done since 20 years, basically it has shifted mobility from oil to coal. It has not decarbonised mobility. It's a bit different.

Akshat Rathi

Yeah, but it has also decreased the carbon intensity in the process because if it was just a switch from oil in the same amount to coal in the same amount, it would be very bad news. But because the electric car is so much more efficient, goes three, four times the difference for the same amount of energy, the total amount of emissions that come out of it are far lower than it would come out of a gasoline-powered car. So it is possible to start to reduce emissions.

I feel like the debate around net-zero is so politically fraught because zero is such an absolute term. That's the title of this podcast. But trying to start to reduce emissions is certainly possible, which is really the debate we should be having at this moment in time. And getting to zero is the final goal, but that does not need you to stop on acting on climate change today.

Jean-Baptiste Fressoz

No, on the contrary, it should encourage you to be much more radical than just imagining putting 1.7 billion electric cars on the roads. I mean, that's basically the energy transition gives people the impression that you can have exactly the same economy without fossil fuels. And this is false. This is not true. I mean, if you really want to tackle the problem of climate change, you really need to change the nature of the economy. You need much less materials, much less cars, much less planes. You need to stop eating meat or beef. I mean, it's really a much more radical thing than what the phrase energy transition conveys.

Akshat Rathi

Yeah. And so this is the part of the book where, trying to write a polemic that changes the conversation, you lay out the problem very well, especially for people who read the book and don't just read the summary and make an assumption about what you're saying. But then towards the end, you don't really have an answer to the problem. Apart from that, you want people to start the conversation that acting on climate change to get to zero is a really radical thing that the world has to do. And we are not talking about that right now.

But at this moment in time, when the politics around climate are so fraught, where the cost of living is the main thing that people care about, where actually shortages of fossil fuels is stopping people, especially in developing countries in Asia, from doing the basic things that they would be able to do. How do you think we can get to a place where there is a space for conversations about the radical idea of getting to zero?

Jean-Baptiste Fressoz

I think we need the scientists to be saying the truth, because the mitigation expertise has really bought ridiculous ideas of negative emissions. As you said, getting to net zero is extremely difficult. So the trick is to say, of course, we know that there will be CO2 from the steel, from the cement, from agriculture, from all sorts of sectors. Nevermind, we will have the capacity to capture CO2 from the air, from the atmosphere and put it under the ground.

And that has really become central in all the net zero scenarios put forward by the IPCC. But this is really a magic trick. The technologies are not ready. They cannot be deployed at scale. If it exists, it will be extraordinarily expensive. It will be also very polluting because you need chemicals to capture CO2. It will be extremely electricity intensive as well. So I think we really need to be saying the truth.

I mean, it's really the objective of scientists to not be to invent the technology that you need to get to net zero just in models. We really need to accept the fact that climate change will happen. We can delay it, sure. But I mean, we can delay it with renewables, with electric cars. But these technologies, they are not really making an energy transition, they are decreasing the carbon intensity of the economy, which is a different thing.

So it is on the table the size of the economy, which should also be part of the discussion. I think for a long time, electric cars would have been called ‘technological progress’ because it is what it is. It is just technological progress. It is less polluting in cities. It is more efficient, as you said, but it's not an energy transition. I mean, let's be a bit serious about the word we use.

Akshat Rathi

And so your next book, I'm assuming, is then Less and Less and Less. The case for why we should be using less of everything.

Jean-Baptiste Fressoz

I mean, I'm a historian, so I don't want to, I do not pretend that there are any solutions in this book. I'm just, I mean, this book shows that the expertise on mitigation is highly problematic. I also trace the origin of the IPCC in the mid-1980s. When the IPCC was created, the point was certainly not to raise the climate agenda. On the contrary, it was to slow down the climate alarm in international circles. The head of IPCC Group 3 was openly climate-sceptic when it was created in 1988.

Akshat Rathi

Yeah, and the Working Group 3 is the group that actually works on reducing emissions. And I found that history very interesting, starting from the 1980s, but also in the 1990s, the amount of influence that the U.S., as a government, had on the mitigation question because they put the Working Group 3 head as somebody who would be essentially taking the message of the U.S. to shape the conversation. But it should be noted that, you know, the IPCC, by the time it wins the Nobel Prize, has gotten rid of that level of influence that it had on its system.

Sure, you can talk about the fact that because they are now more constrained with where emissions are, if they are to meet the Paris Goals, which is what governments have set, the only way they can do it is to have these negative emissions added to the system. So at least the bias on trying to slow down acting on climate change has gone away. But then there is this creation of a technology that really is not yet being costed out, has not been shown to work at scale. That makes it a problem.

You also, as a historian, you said, you know, I look at history to try and help you have a conversation. But, you know, you frame in the book that the use of energy transition as a futurology has gone wrong. You know, can you explain why futurologies matter so much? Because currently there's a narrative that actually the world has become far too short-termist, with politicians only thinking in the three to five year political cycle, or company CEOs only thinking quarter to quarter.

And, you know, in some cases, and this is true because I've spoken to lots of these young people, there's a nihilistic conversation that there is no future anyway. So like, you know, what the hell? But you still think that futurologies matter. Why?

Jean-Baptiste Fressoz

I mean, the idea of energy transition has always been a futurology. It will happen in the future, right? In the 1970s, William Nordhaus introduced the idea of backstop technology. There is a backstop technology for the energy crisis. Then it is used in climate economics. There is a backstop technology for climate change. First it is the Breeder reactor and then it became the negative emissions I just mentioned.

So I think we have discourse, which is really future orientated. It is orientated toward the future. It is the future that will save us from climate change. Technology from the future that will save us from climate change. Whereas we could have a completely different discussion saying that it's probable that the world, or the technological world, won't be that different in 20 years. I think it's a fair assumption to make. I mean, of course there will be more electric cars, there will be more renewable in the electricity mix, but it won't be a fundamentally different world.

So what do we do with the technology we have? Instead of dreaming about the innovations coming to save our beans.

Akshat Rathi

But one way to think about the More and More and More framing is also that in this time period, the population of the world has been growing and growing and growing. And we now know that that is likely to peak and start to fall. And these are very credible analyses done by lots of people, but including by the United Nations. And for the first time, the world will be different because the entire human population will start to shrink. Don't you think there is a chance that the energy transition is a very different conversation in a shrinking population?

Jean-Baptiste Fressoz

I mean, what matters is not that much the number of people, but the way they live. And I think even if there is a shrinking of the global population, the forecast are rather that the global GDP will grow in the next 20 years. So I think that's really the key factor. It is rather the way you live and not the number of people. I mean, for global population, the only good effect could be the slowdown of deforestation, which is a major factor of emissions.

And we always talk about energies and fanciful technologies, but we just have to take in mind that one third of global emissions is the global food system. From deforestation to the management of waste, through fertilisers and packaging, refrigeration, all sorts of things. I mean, here, once again, we could talk about energy transition for certain small aspects of this. Like refrigeration, I guess you can do better, of course, than using fossil fuels.

Nevertheless, the dynamics is not one of transition. If you want to be really serious about this big chunk of emissions, one third of emissions, once again, you need to talk about reducing certain consumption. And I think that's really something we need to keep in mind, because it shows that this idea of energy transition is completely disconnected from the global atmosphere.

It addresses just a small part of the problem, like the electricity production mainly, which is 40% of emissions. But for the rest, once again, I don't think this is the right word to use.

Akshat Rathi

But I think you are also only looking at one point of view and you are saying that is why we need to reduce. But there is a possibility, which now in the scenarios that are there to reduce emissions, yes, GDP continues to grow. Like all the climate models assume GDP growth alongside reduction in emissions. And so why is it a problem if that were possible? Why is it a problem that the world continues to consume more if they are able to reduce emissions and get to net zero? Because there are credible scenarios that allow you to do that while economic growth is happening. Why is that a problem?

Jean-Baptiste Fressoz

I mean, the problem is that they are not credible at all. They are rubbish. They are founded on false technological promises. The negative emissions I mentioned, they are key, really. I mean, without negative emissions, no scenario can get to carbon neutrality.

Akshat Rathi

But reductions in emissions are real, right? Like 90% reduction is still possible with the technologies that are available to us, or 80% reduction is possible. It's the last 10 to 20% is what really the negative emissions are used for today, in the scenarios.

Jean-Baptiste Fressoz

I mean, we are at around 10 gigatons of negative emissions for net-zero. So it's more like, yeah, 25%.

Akshat Rathi

20%.

Jean-Baptiste Fressoz

Yeah. But even like the reduction you said, it's really by being very optimistic on the technologies that people like.

Akshat Rathi

Leaving aside the economic system or the scenarios or the conversation around energy transition, there's a real political question around consuming less that needs to be addressed. And right now that will involve questions around inequality, around injustice, around North-South divide, around colonialism, around extractivism that has been going on for hundreds of years. Those are questions that almost no forum is ready to discuss and find a solution for.

How exactly can we get to a point of having that discussion? Because without that, we don't get to zero. We might get to 80% reduction, but getting to zero is what we really need to do eventually, right?

Jean-Baptiste Fressoz

What I would want to see is economists starting to explain what is the welfare associated with certain emissions of CO2. There will be CO2 emissions in the economy after 2050. That is absolutely certain. The key question should be to do what? For whom? And to do that, we need to get out of the dollar metrics or the monetary metrics. We need to really understand welfare. I'll just give you an example. Cement.

Cement is extraordinarily difficult to decarbonise because you need to do carbon capture and storage and I don't think it works very well. So cement is very difficult to decarbonise, but it can be used to build a new skyscraper in London. I'm not sure there is so much welfare into that, even if there is probably a lot of money to be made. Or it can be used to make a water network in a megalopolis of the poor world, where there is a really sudden increase in welfare when you've got running water at your home.

So I think it is this kind of discussion that we need to have. It's, I mean, the economists can help us to have the discussion by studying that properly. But in the end, it will be a democratic discussion. It will be about which country accepts which kind of sacrifice, you know, and I think we need to have the discussion.

For me, the issue of climate change is really an issue about redistribution. If we really want to reduce CO2 emissions in the short term, like in the next 20 years, of course, we can decarbonise electricity, but that's just part of the work. Then we have to reduce the consumption of rich people in the first place. And that is indispensable if you want to make room for the growth of the poor world, which is going to be enormous in the next decades.

So I think the problem of climate-change expertise is that it didn't want to touch upon this very tricky issue of inequalities. And the net zero scenarios. They do not envisage the GDP of rich countries to decrease, for instance, which is a very strange hypothesis when you think about it. If you really want to get to net zero, you could say, OK, let's start where it is easy to decrease emissions, like the US or Australia or Saudi Arabia, or whatever countries where they emit really a lot and they have a very high GDP.

But this has never been studied, which is amazing when you think about it. It has been 30 years that these economists have been working on climate change and that they have not started to study that.

Akshat Rathi

This was fun. I hope the next history book you do is the history of how climate scenarios were shaped and why we make the assumptions that we do. Because, yeah, we certainly need more scenarios to understand how the world could be shaped. Thank you, Jean-Baptiste.

Jean-Baptiste Fressoz

Thanks for having me.

Akshat Rathi

And thank you for listening to Zero. Now for the sound of the week. That’s the sound of kickoff at the opening game of this year’s football world cup, played between Mexico and South Africa. 48 countries will play at this year’s world cup, the highest ever, and matches will take place across Mexico, Canada and the USA… making it the world cup with the most greenhouse gas emissions too.

If you liked this episode, please take a moment to rate and review the show on Apple Podcasts, YouTube, and Spotify. This episode was produced by Oscar Boyd. Our theme music is composed by Wonderly. Special thanks to Sommer Saadi, Laura Millan, and Sharon Chen. I'm Akshat Rathi. Back soon.
item.dc:creator: Akshat Rathi, Oscar Boyd

item.title: European Heat Wave Threatens to Spur Record Cooling Demand
item.description:
Western Europe is baking under a heat wave that threatens to drive record cooling demand, just as warming rivers force French nuclear reactors to curb output.

The heat wave is forecast to push temperatures 5C to 12C above normal in France, Germany, Italy, Spain and southern England, which could raise cooling demand to the highest level in records going back 45 years, according to analysis from Vaisala meteorologist Matthew Dross.

The unusual heat is driven by a dome of high pressure that raises temperatures as it compresses air toward the ground and clears away clouds. Daytime highs could reach above 40C (104F) by Sunday in Paris and other regions, according to forecaster Météo-France.

The most intense heat is forecast for France, where authorities have issued amber weather alerts across 26 departments. That includes Paris and much of the central and eastern parts of the country, where grain fields are almost ready for harvest.

Météo-France warned that the heat wave coincides with the annual Fete de la Musique on June 21, when concerts and street parties are held across the country. Schools across Paris and other regions have announced that classes will be suspended in the afternoon on Thursday and Friday because of the heat wave, according to Agence France-Presse.

The unusually long-lasting heat wave threatens to curb nuclear output from plants cooled by the Rhône and Garonne rivers, including Saint-Alban, where Electricité de France SA is preparing to limit generation from Saturday. EDF warned that its Blayais and Golfech facilities may also be impacted from June 23 and June 25, respectively.

French nuclear output is closely watched by power markets since the country’s reactor fleet is the backbone of Europe’s electricity system. Restrictions can tighten supplies and lift prices across the region.

French power demand rose on Thursday to about 52.4 gigawatts, the highest level since April 14, according to data from RTE.

To reduce the risk of air-conditioning failures during the heat wave, French transport company SNCF has canceled more than 70 train services on Thursday and Friday, including 14 Paris–Limoges–Toulouse trains, according to France Info.

The heat wave is likely to further dry soils, which have already been depleted by low rainfall in June and a similar heat wave in late May, said Météo-France. Some areas are approaching record-low moisture levels for the season over the coming days.

The combination of dry vegetation and hot weather with low humidity has raised wildfire risks in France and Spain.

Heat wave conditions are also forecast for Germany and are likely to spread into southern England over the weekend, according to the UK Met Office. Temperatures could reach 33C in some areas, with 31C possible in London on Monday and Tuesday. Amber heat health alerts are in place for London and southern England.

Amber heat warnings have also been issued for Switzerland, with a red alert for the northern region around the city of Basel. There are amber warnings in Italy and Spain, along with yellow alerts for Austria, Belgium, Germany and the Netherlands.
item.dc:creator: Joe Wertz, Nayla Razzouk

item.title: Nvidia-Backed Startup Aims to Speed AI Data Center Grid Connections
item.description:
Long queues to get connected to the strained energy grid have sent data centers scrambling for ways to get power fast — from hauling in natural gas turbines to revamping how data centers are designed.

Verse Enterprises Inc., a San Francisco-based startup, says its software can help data centers skip the line by managing off-grid batteries and solar.

The company already makes software that analyzes energy pricing and demand so facilities can monitor their energy consumption. Now it’s partnering with battery developer Calibrant Energy to incorporate storage and solar generation into the equation, a setup it says can avoid overloading the grid during peak demand.

It’s betting this will convince utilities to take on data centers’ extra load without making costly and time-consuming infrastructure upgrades.

“We're now at the point that clean energy is actually the economic and feasible solution because the speed of deployment is fast,” Verse’s co-founder and Chief Executive Officer Seyed Madaeni said.

The company raised $54 million this spring in a Series B round that included chipmaking giant Nvidia Corp. as a backer. It’s part of an effort by AI companies to fund energy-efficiency startups that can help them cut their rapidly growing power use. Nvidia has also invested millions in Emerald AI, which makes a similar software.

Read more: The Race to Rethink Data Centers for AI’s Power Surge

But installing these systems isn’t a guarantee that utility companies will hook up data centers right away, according to Allison Weis, global head of storage at Wood Mackenzie. Flexibility in power demand can reduce barriers to getting connected, but utilities haven’t defined any standards for data centers to meet to get online, or said that batteries or on-site power generation will help fulfill them.

“There’s no uniform framework,” she said.

Federal regulators are set to unveil guidance on how the US will expedite data center connections. At issue is whether hyperscalers will be responsible for providing their own power generation, commit to flexible energy consumption or foot the bill for grid upgrades.

Falling costs and rising electricity needs have led to a boom in battery deployment. The energy storage market is forecast to grow 21% this year as demand for batteries grows despite tariffs and restrictions on tax credits for batteries that rely heavily on Chinese components.

Verse said it’s using the raise to scale deployment of their technology to more than 100 sites by early next year.
item.dc:creator: Summer Maxwell

item.title: Top EU Party Wants Carbon Market Changes to Protect Industries
item.description:
The biggest political group in the European Union’s parliament is considering pushing for carbon market changes to safeguard the region’s industrial competitiveness and curb excessive price swings.

The EU’s Emissions Trading System has moved up the bloc’s political agenda ahead of planned reforms to the program that are due to be unveiled in July. The ETS is a key tool to curb greenhouse gases, but some governments and energy intensive sectors have criticized it for contributing to high energy costs.

The European People’s Party “fully backs” the carbon market, but wants a number of adjustments to ensure an “economically sustainable” shift away from fossil fuels, according to a draft internal document seen by Bloomberg. That includes a two-step approach to slowing the pace of emissions reductions and overhauling a mechanism that controls supply in the carbon market.

“We want to provide breathing space for industry and guarantee value creation in the EU,” the group said in the document.

Started in 2005, the ETS imposes gradually shrinking emissions limits on around 10,000 facilities in sectors from steel to cement to fertilizers. The European Commission is due to unveil a review of the market on July 15 to adjust it to a new climate goal of reducing emissions by 90% by 2040 from 1990 levels.

Once published, the review will be discussed by the European Parliament and member states in the EU Council, with each institution having the right to propose amendments.

The EPP wants to slow the rate at which emission limits fall each year, known as the Linear Reduction Factor, to avoid the cap dropping to zero in 2039 under the system’s current design, according to the document. It should be lowered by at least 1 percentage point between 2031 and 2035, followed by “even more moderate trajectory after 2035, subject to an assessment of industrial competitiveness, technological progress and infrastructure readiness,” it said.

The party also wants to overhaul the Market Stability Reserve, which automatically controls supply in the ETS, to make it more responsive. The focus needs to be on preventing excessive price swings, the EPP said in the document.

The bloc should also establish a market for CO2 removals — where allowance are created for removing carbon from the atmosphere — and make them part of the ETS too, the EPP’s document shows. International carbon credits should be part of the EU emissions market as well, the party said.

A spokesperson for the EPP declined to comment.

The EPP’s other calls include:

  • Slowing the shift away from free allowances during the rollout of the Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism — a tool that puts a carbon price on imported goods — with a maximum phaseout of 30% before 2030.
  • Industries not covered by CBAM must have access to free permits beyond 2030.
  • Revising the methodology for carbon efficiency benchmarks, which determine how many free permits facilities get.
  • Companies that decarbonize faster than others should be rewarded with free permits for longer.
  • Member states must spend their ETS revenues on clean investments, and ETS-based funds that benefit the environment should be strengthened.
  • The EU should keep carbon charges on airlines to intra-European routes, rather than to expand them to flights into and out of the region.
item.dc:creator: Ewa Krukowska

item.title: Australia Sees Biggest Jump in Planned Large Renewable Projects
item.description:
The amount of large-scale renewables projects classified as “probable” in Australia rose the most in records going back a decade, as the country pushes forward with ambitious plans to reduce emissions and hit green targets.

These types of projects rose about a third to 32.3 gigawatts in the week ending May 29, according to data compiled by Australia’s clean energy regulator. That takes the total large-scale renewables pipeline up to almost 70 gigawatts, or more than triple the capacity of the coal plants that have historically generated most of the nation’s power.

The increase comes after Australia’s government in May approved about 10 gigawatts of wind and solar projects, as well as battery facilities, as part of public tenders.

Australia has become a bellwether for the energy transition, as it seeks to replace its rapidly aging fleet of coal power stations and meet a goal of renewables achieving 82% of total generation by 2030. That compares with 46.5% in the main grid in the first quarter, a record for the period despite unprecedented demand, according to the market operator.

Still, there is no guarantee that these proposed solar and wind facilities will ultimately come to fruition, while the gap between probable developments and accredited or committed projects continues to widen. Among the biggest hurdles facing new projects are transmission infrastructure delays and lengthy planning and permitting processes.
item.dc:creator: Stephen Stapczynski

item.title: Data Centers Are Being Built in Areas Exposed to Extreme Weather
item.description:
A significant number of data centers is being built in areas that are exposed to extreme weather risks brought on by climate change, a report by XDI has found.

A study of roughly 2,600 planned data centers worldwide indicates that more than 150 will be on high-risk properties, according to the analysis by XDI, which looks at physical climate impacts. Key hazards include coastal inundation, high heat and riverine flooding, it said.

“We are not adequately preparing for climate change,” Karl Mallon, founder and head of science and technology at XDI, said in a press briefing ahead of the report’s release on Thursday.

More than 70 of the roughly 150 high-risk data centers are set to be built in North America, compared with 45 in Europe and 12 in East Asia, the analysis found.

Read More: Data Center Alley Is Reshaping the Northeast Carbon Market

In the UK, rain-induced flooding is the main threat to data centers intended to train large AI models, according to a separate report published by the MSCI Institute. Higher temperatures are also a concern. In 2022, London data centers used by Google and Oracle Corp. buckled under the effect of record heat, knocking some websites offline.

The XDI report also cautioned against underestimating indirect risks. “A data center designed to remain operational during an extreme weather event may still fail if surrounding infrastructure is not,” it said.

Asset owners still have an opportunity to offset such risks. Decisions about “site selection, engineering standards and resilience investment may materially influence” the climate resilience of such infrastructure, XDI said.

“A huge chunk of global capital is going into this area,” Mallon said. “If we get this wrong it will be a very expensive wrong.”
item.dc:creator: Gautam Naik

item.title: Jera Books Mitsui OSK Ships to Bring Low-Carbon Ammonia to Japan
item.description:
Jera Co. has agreed to book two gas carriers from Mitsui OSK Lines Ltd. that will enable Japan’s largest power producer to transport low-carbon ammonia from the US and build up a supply chain for the alternative fuel.

MOL, one of the world’s largest shipping companies, said the vessels will move the fuel from the Blue Point project in Louisiana to Jera’s thermal power station in the city of Hekinan, in Aichi prefecture. Shipments will begin around Japan’s 2029 fiscal year.

The contract “marks a major step forward in MOL’s initiative to develop a marine transport system for low-carbon ammonia,” the company said in a statement Thursday, adding that it had been in discussions with Jera on transport of the fuel since November 2022.

Read More: Japan Offers Energy Loan, Ammonia Technology to South Africa

Low-carbon ammonia has been receiving greater attention around the world as governments and companies pledge to reduce emissions from fossil fuels. Japan has been promoting a technology known as ammonia co-firing, where a portion of the coal used by power plants is replaced to curb emissions of climate-warming gases.

In recent years, the country has offered to help other nations curb emissions through the use of co-firing technology. Japanese trading house Marubeni Corp. last year signed a deal with Exxon Mobil Corp. to take about 250,000 tons of the fuel a year once a project in Baytown, Texas is up and running.
item.dc:creator: Sing Yee Ong
